does: This paper introduces weak-critic strong oversight, a framework where a weaker model provides constructive revisions rather than labels or judgments. The authors develop progressive on-policy critique distillation (OPCD), which filters high-quality critiques from a weak critic and distills them into a strong model through adaptive self-teacher signals. The method operates both at inference time—where weak critiques help frozen strong models improve predictions—and during training, progressively refining the strong model across epochs. Experiments on reasoning and alignment benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements, showing that weak critiques can meaningfully boost strong model performance when filtered for quality and properly integrated through self-teaching mechanisms.
connects: This work builds on recent research in scalable oversight and weak-to-strong generalization, where strong models must learn from imperfect supervision due to human capability limitations on complex tasks. It extends beyond prior approaches that use weak models as labelers or final judges by shifting to a critique-based paradigm, drawing parallels to human feedback methods and constitutional AI where models learn from directional guidance rather than binary labels. The paper connects to broader research on self-improvement and iterative refinement, as well as distillation approaches that transfer knowledge through model-to-model learning. It responds to fundamental challenges in aligning increasingly capable models when human evaluators cannot reliably judge their outputs.
enables: This framework opens pathways for practical scalable oversight of advanced language models where human or weak-model supervision becomes increasingly insufficient. The approach suggests that future oversight systems need not rely on perfect external judges but can leverage strategic weak feedback to enhance strong models' self-correction and knowledge synthesis. It points toward hybrid human-AI evaluation loops where weak critics—whether human annotators, smaller models, or specialized verifiers—work synergistically with strong models. The OPCD method could enable more efficient training of aligned models by reducing dependence on expensive human annotations, potentially scaling oversight to increasingly complex reasoning and decision-making tasks while maintaining quality through adaptive filtering and self-teaching mechanisms.
